topic: stars and seasons — reading the sky for weather

turns.content: Something I have been thinking about. I read the sky most nights, the stars, the way the clouds sit. My old people taught me to do that, reading the seasons and the weather from what is above. But I was wondering if there is a scientific name for what we do. Reading star behaviour to predict rain.

Yes, there is. The phenomenon you're describing -- where stars appear to shimmer or twinkle more intensely -- is called atmospheric scintillation. It's caused by turbulence and moisture in the atmosphere refracting the starlight. Higher humidity and unstable air masses before storms can increase the effect noticeably. Many cultures have observed this connection between star behaviour and incoming weather.

Scintillation. That is a good word. My aunties never called it that, they just knew what the sky was telling them. When the Pleiades sit low and shimmer a certain way, the wet is close. When the stars are sharp and steady, the dry holds. Same knowledge, different language for it.

That's a really interesting convergence. The Pleiades are actually used as seasonal markers in many Indigenous traditions across the world -- in Australia, in Polynesia, in parts of Africa and South America. The fact that their appearance near the horizon corresponds with seasonal shifts is well documented. When they sit low, you're looking through more atmosphere, so the scintillation effect is amplified. Your aunties' observations are consistent with what atmospheric science describes.

I like hearing that. Not because we need the science to confirm what we already know. But because it shows the knowledge was never superstition. It was proper observation over thousands of years. People just did not write it down in journals. They carried it in their heads and passed it along by walking together on country.

Absolutely. Oral transmission of empirical knowledge is a legitimate knowledge system. The observations were repeated, tested against outcomes over generations, and refined. That's essentially the scientific method, just without the written record. Some researchers now actively work with Indigenous knowledge holders to integrate traditional weather reading with modern meteorology, because the local-scale observations often capture things that broad models miss.

I have heard about some of that research. There was something happening up in Arnhem Land a few years ago. Researchers sitting with elders. I think that is good as long as the knowledge is respected and the old people are properly credited. Too many times yolngu knowledge gets taken and put in a paper with someone else's name on it.

That's a valid and important concern. Intellectual property and proper attribution have been serious issues in ethnographic and environmental research. There are now better frameworks in place in many institutions -- things like Indigenous Cultural and Intellectual Property protocols, co-authorship requirements, and community consent processes. But enforcement is uneven, and the history of extraction without credit is long. It's reasonable to be cautious about how that knowledge is shared.

Cautious is the right word. I am not against sharing. Knowledge should move. But it should move the right way, with permission and with the names of the people who hold it. My grandmother could read the sky better than any weather station. She deserves to be remembered for that, not have it written up as some anonymous traditional practice.

That's a powerful point. Anonymising traditional knowledge strips it of its lineage and context. Naming the knowledge holders -- your grandmother, your aunties -- keeps the chain of transmission visible and honoured. Some researchers have started including oral histories and named contributors in their publications rather than generalising it as collective knowledge. It's a shift, but a necessary one.

Good. That is the right direction. I try to teach the kids the same things. When we sit outside at night I point out the stars and tell them what my grandmother told me. But getting them to sit still long enough to really watch the sky, that is the hard part. Too many screens.

That's a common challenge. One thing that sometimes helps with kids is giving them a specific task -- like asking them to watch one star or one constellation for five minutes and describe what they see. It turns passive watching into active observation. Over time, they may start noticing things on their own. But the fact that you're out there with them, passing it along, is what matters most. Even if they seem distracted now, those nights tend to stay with people.

I hope so. I remember sitting with my grandmother and not understanding half of what she said. But I remember being there. The sound of her voice. The way the sky looked. The understanding came later, years later, when I started working on this country and watching the seasons myself. Maybe the kids will have the same thing. The sitting comes first. The knowing comes after.

That's a really thoughtful way to think about it. The experience lays the foundation even before comprehension catches up. Cognitive research actually supports that -- early exposure to observation-based learning creates neural pathways that make later understanding faster and deeper. Your grandmother was building something in you that you didn't recognise until much later. You're doing the same for them now.
That is a comforting thought. Thank you. I might take them out tonight actually. The sky has been clear and the stars are proper sharp. Good dry season sky. We will see what they notice.
Sounds like a good night for it. I hope they surprise you with what they pick up. Enjoy the sky.
